title: Psalms 87
subtitle: Glorious Things Are Said of You, City of God

sections.header: Verses 1–3 — “He Has Founded His City on the Holy Mountain”

heb.paragraph: "He has founded his city on the holy mountain. The LORD loves the gates of Zion (Ṣiyyōn) more than all the other dwellings of Jacob. Glorious things are said of you, city of God" (vv.1–3). A Zion hymn. God loves Zion above all places. Glorious things are SAID — the city’s reputation precedes it.

notes.note: "Glorious things are said of you, city of God." Three verses, pure praise. The city’s glory is spoken of; its reputation is global.

refs.note: The Jerusalem above as our mother develops the psalm's Zion-as-birthplace theology.
Coming to Mount Zion, the heavenly Jerusalem, fulfills the psalm's glorification of the city.
The new Jerusalem coming down from heaven completes the psalm's vision.

hist.context: Psalm 87 is a Korahite Zion song with unusual features: it anticipates Gentiles being registered as Zion-born. Rahab (Egypt), Babylon, Philistia, Tyre, and Cush will say 'I was born in Zion.' This remarkable universalism claims that nations traditionally hostile to Israel will become citizens of the holy city.

sections.header: Verses 4–7 — “This One Was Born in Zion”

heb.paragraph: "This one was born (yullad) in Zion" (v.6). The psalm’s most remarkable claim: God will register the nations as having been born in Zion. Rahab (Egypt), Babylon, Philistia, Tyre, Cush — "this one was born in Zion." Gentile nations will be enrolled as Zion’s citizens. Birth-right in God’s city is not by ethnicity but by divine registration.

notes.note: "I will record Rahab and Babylon among those who acknowledge me. This one was born in Zion." The nations become citizens of Zion. The most inclusive vision of the OT: enemies enrolled as family.
"As they make music they will sing, ‘All my fountains are in you.’" The nations’ song: Zion is the source of life. The fountains are spiritual; the citizenship is real.

refs.note: No longer foreigners but fellow citizens fulfills the psalm's inclusive citizenship vision.
Citizenship in heaven develops the psalm's register-of-peoples concept.
The great multitude from every nation before the throne fulfills Zion's universal embrace.

hist.context: The divine registry (v.6: 'the LORD will write in the register of the peoples') records birth certificates. Being 'born in Zion' confers citizenship regardless of ethnic origin. The psalm's joyful conclusion — 'all my fountains are in you' (v.7) — suggests celebration through music and dance, with Zion as the source of life.

ppl.name: God
ppl.text: "Glorious things are said of you, city of God…

chapter_panels.trans: VersionTranslationNIVGlorious things are said of you, city of God:ESVGlorious things of you are spoken, O city of God. SelahNIVThe Lord will write in the register of the peoples: “This one was born in Zion.”ESVThe LORD records as he registers the peoples, \u201cThis one was born there.\u201d Selah

rec.text: ""Glorious things are said of you, city of God." Three verses, pure praise. The city’s glory is spoken of; its reputation is global."

rows.label: The City — 87:1–3
rows.text: "Glorious things are said of you, city of God." God loves Zion’s gates.

hebtext.note: "He has founded his city on the holy mountain. The LORD loves the gates of Zion (Ṣiyyōn) more than all the other dwellings of Jacob. Glorious things are said of you, city of God" (vv.1–3). A Zion hymn. God loves Zion above all places. Glorious things are SAID — the city’s reputation precedes it.

"This one was born (yullad) in Zion" (v.6). The psalm’s most remarkable claim: God will register the nations as having been born in Zion. Rahab (Egypt), Babylon, Philistia, Tyre, Cush — "this one was born in Zion." Gentile nations will be enrolled as Zion’s citizens. Birth-right in God’s city is not by ethnicity but by divine registration.

themes.note: Mission peaks: Gentile nations enrolled as Zion’s citizens. Prophecy: universal citizenship in God’s city. Covenant: birth-right by divine registration.
